The Cathedral and the Coffee Shop

Why the best teams build like a cathedral and ship like a coffee shop, and how to keep both alive at the same time.

--

Listen

Share

I have been thinking about how teams ship software, and I keep coming back to two metaphors that seem like opposites but actually need each other.

The Cathedral

A cathedral is built over decades. The people who lay the foundation will not see the spire. Every stone is placed with the assumption that someone you have never met will need to maintain it long after you are gone. That is a powerful constraint, and it produces a particular kind of beauty: load-bearing decisions made carefully, on purpose, with future readers in mind.

The best codebases I have ever worked in feel a little like this. You can read a function and understand not just what it does, but why it exists. The names are stable. The boundaries are deliberate. The tests describe behavior, not implementation. Walking through it feels like wandering a building someone took the time to design.

What Cathedral Code Looks Like

A few signals I look for:

  • Module boundaries that reflect domain concepts, not file-system convenience.
  • Tests that survive a refactor without changes, because they test the contract.
  • Names a new hire can read out loud and immediately guess at the meaning of.
// Cathedral: the contract is the API.
function applyDiscount(order, code) {
  const rule = discountRules.find((r) => r.matches(order, code));
  return rule ? rule.apply(order) : order;
}

The Coffee Shop

A coffee shop is the opposite. It opens at six. The espresso machine is loud. Someone is going to ask for oat milk in a way that makes you reconsider your career. The whole operation is optimized to respond: to a customer, to a spill, to a sudden rush at 8:15. There is no time for cathedrals at 8:15. You hand someone a cup or you do not.

The best teams I have worked on can do this too. A bug report comes in, someone reproduces it within the hour, the fix is in main by lunch, and a customer who was about to churn is still a customer at the end of the day. The work is small, fast, and unceremonious. It is a coffee shop, not a cathedral.

When Coffee Shop Mode Wins

Reach for coffee shop mode when:

  1. A real user is bleeding right now, and the cost of a small inelegance is lower than the cost of waiting.
  2. You are at the edge of the system, in glue code that nobody is going to read again.
  3. The change is reversible, observable, and small enough to fit in your head.
// Coffee shop: just unblock the user.
if (order.couponCode === "LEGACY10") {
  order.total *= 0.9;
}

Holding Both at Once

The mistake is to pick one and call it your style. Cathedral-only teams ship slowly and feel righteous about it. Coffee-shop-only teams ship quickly and wake up to a codebase nobody can reason about. Neither is sustainable.

The teams I admire most do both, and they are honest about which mode they are in. They write the load-bearing parts like a cathedral and they patch the edges like a coffee shop, and they know the difference. There is a longer version of this argument elsewhere, but the short version is: tell yourself, out loud, which mode you are in before you start typing.

If there is one thing to take away: the best engineers I know are bilingual. They can build a cathedral on Tuesday and run a coffee shop on Wednesday, and they do not feel guilty about either one.
